title: Gold & Silver Slip as Dollar Strengthens: 4 Stocks to Own
content:
Gold and silver prices are not moving the way many investors would have expected lately. With geopolitical tensions heating up, precious metals would normally be rallying strongly. Instead, both gold and silver prices witnessed a slight pullback yesterday, likely due to a surge in the U.S. dollar and Treasury yields. When the dollar strengthens, commodities priced in the currency automatically become more expensive for global buyers. That tends to cool demand in the short term, which is what we are seeing in both gold and silver.

Higher bond yields are also drawing some money away from precious metals. Unlike bonds, gold and silver do not generate income. When yields climb, investors sometimes rotate toward fixed-income assets that offer a predictable return.

Expectations around interest rates have also played a role. Analysts at Goldman Sachs recently pushed back their forecast for Fed rate cuts, now expecting quarter-point reductions only in September and December. If interest rates remain elevated for longer, it could impact the demand for non-yielding assets like gold and silver.

Even so, the broader outlook for precious metals remains constructive. Stocks like Agnico Eagle Mines AEM, Iamgold IAG, First Majestic Silver AG and Buenaventura Mining BVN are solid bets to ride the gold and silver theme.  Each of these stocks sports a Zacks Rank #1 (Strong Buy).

You can see the complete list of today’s Zacks #1 Rank stocks here.

The Long-Term Story Still Looks Strong

The recent decline in gold and silver prices looks more like a pause after a strong rally rather than the start of a downturn.

Gold continues to benefit from steady central-bank buying and persistent geopolitical risks. Tensions in the Middle East— including concerns involving Iran and uncertainty around shipments through the Strait of Hormuz—are key drivers of safe-haven demand if market volatility increases.

Silver, meanwhile, has an additional tailwind from industrial demand. The global solar industry consumes enormous quantities of the metal for photovoltaic cells, while the rapid build-out of artificial-intelligence infrastructure and advanced electronics is adding another layer of demand.

Investors are looking ahead to the upcoming Federal Reserve policy meeting scheduled for March 17-18. Any signal that interest rates may start falling earlier than expected could provide fresh support for precious metals.

With the long-term safe-haven narrative intact, any near-term pullback in gold and silver may offer investors an opportunity to build exposure — whether through physical metals, ETFs or mining stocks. Below are four stocks that could benefit from the strong long-term fundamentals of gold and silver.

Story Continues

4 Top Stocks to Buy

Agnico Eagle remains one of the most reliable names in the gold mining space, supported by a strong pipeline of growth projects and healthy cash generation. The company is advancing several key assets, including the Odyssey project at the Canadian Malartic Complex, Detour Lake, Hope Bay, Upper Beaver and San Nicolas. Among these, Hope Bay stands out with proven and probable reserves of about 3.4 million ounces, which could meaningfully support production and cash flows in the coming years. Agnico Eagle also maintains a solid balance sheet and liquidity position. Strong cash flows allow the company to invest in exploration, fund growth projects and reward shareholders through dividends and buybacks.

The Zacks Consensus Estimate for AEM’s 2026 sales and EPS implies year-over-year growth of 38% and 60%, respectively.

IAMGOLD is focused on strengthening its production base while advancing a pipeline of growth projects. The company continues to optimize operations at its key mines, including Essakane, which remains an important cash-flow contributor. At the Côté gold operation, management is working to reduce unit costs while preparing for a future expansion that could unlock additional value from the combined Côté and Gosselin deposits. IAMGOLD is also advancing exploration at the Nelligan Complex. The asset is already shaping up to be one of Canada’s largest pre-production gold projects. Strong operating performance has also enabled the company to reduce debt and return capital to shareholders through share buybacks.

The Zacks Consensus Estimate for IAG’s 2026 EPS has moved up 27 cents to $2.14 over the past 30 days, implying year-over-year growth of 74%

First Majestic is a leading silver and gold miner with operations primarily in Mexico and the United States. The company currently runs four producing mines— Santa Elena, San Dimas, Los Gatos and La Encantada— while also advancing development assets such as the Jerritt Canyon project in Nevada. Production growth has been strong last year, helped by the acquisition of the Los Gatos mine, which significantly boosted silver output. Existing operations have also delivered higher volumes. First Majestic plans to invest in expanding key mines, advance development projects and carry out extensive exploration drilling, supporting future resource growth and long-term production potential.

The Zacks Consensus Estimate for AG’s 2026 EPS has moved up 26 cents to 60 cents over the past 30 days, implying year-over-year growth of 30%

Buenaventura is one of Peru’s leading precious-metals producers, with a portfolio of gold and silver mines across the country. The company fully owns several operations, including Orcopampa, Uchucchacua, Julcani, Tambomayo and La Zanja, providing solid exposure to precious-metal production. BVN also holds majority and minority stakes in other mining ventures, including El Brocal and Coimolache, while its stake in Sociedad Minera Cerro Verde offers additional diversification through copper. The recently commissioned San Gabriel project is emerging as a key growth driver, expected to gradually ramp up production and help offset declining output from older mines while supporting Buenaventura’s long-term production outlook.

The Zacks Consensus Estimate for BVN’s 2026 sales and EPS implies year-over-year growth of 16% and 18%, respectively.

title: IAG, easyJet and Ryanair; which European airlines are investors selling as Iran burns?
content:
IAG, easyJet and Ryanair; which European airlines are investors selling as Iran burns? Proactive uses images sourced from Shutterstock

Investors have been repositioning sharply across European airline stocks since the outbreak of Middle East hostilities, with Ryanair Holdings PLC (LSE:RYA) emerging as the clear defensive favourite while short positions have built against carriers more vulnerable to elevated fuel prices.

This is Citi's analysis of positioning data from institutional investors, with the analysis showing that sector positioning has turned more negative over both the past week and month.

Investors have increased short exposure in Wizz Air Holdings PLC (AIM:WIZZ), easyJet PLC (LSE:EZJ) and Air France-KLM, which Citi characterises as the most operationally and financially leveraged carriers and therefore most exposed to sustained fuel price pressure.

British Airways owner International Consolidated Airlines Group SA (LSE:IAG) has also seen a shift, with long positioning that had been particularly heavy now moving to a more balanced level as investors take profits or hedge against further volatility.

Ryanair has bucked the trend, with long positioning increasing over the past week as investors gravitate toward what Citi describes as the carrier best equipped to weather near-term geopolitical turmoil. This view, the bank said, is "widely and rightly held".

The Irish carrier's low-cost model, strong hedging position and predominantly intra-European network make it less exposed than peers to Middle Eastern route disruptions and transatlantic demand softness.

Lufthansa is a notable outlier in the data. While positioning has turned more negative over the past month, that shift predates the onset of the conflict, despite the German carrier having particularly high exposure to crack spreads, the margin between crude oil and refined jet fuel prices that has widened sharply since hostilities began.

Citi said near-term share prices across the sector would remain heavily dictated by fuel price movements.

title: iA Financial Group Announces Leadership Transition at Richardson Wealth: Julie Gallagher to Be Appointed as President and Chief Executive Officer
content:
TORONTO, March 10, 2026--(BUSINESS WIRE)--iA Financial Group today announced the appointment of Julie Gallagher as President and Chief Executive Officer, Richardson Wealth, effective immediately. A seasoned and highly respected financial services leader, Ms. Gallagher, will provide strategic direction and vision, drive growth and profitability, and continue to strengthen the firm’s support for its advisory teams.

On October 31, 2025, iA Financial Group completed the acquisition of RF Capital Group Inc. ("RF Capital"), a leading independent wealth management company, operating under the Richardson Wealth brand. Dave Kelly, outgoing President and CEO of RF Capital, will remain with the organization until the end of June in the capacity of Vice-Chair to help Julie Gallagher during her transition period. Beyond that, he will retain a seat on the Board of Directors of Richardson Wealth continuing to provide guidance and counsel going forward.

"As we continue our expansion in the wealth management space, Julie possesses precisely the right combination of skills, experience and continuity we need," said Stephan Bourbonnais, Executive Vice‑President, iA Wealth. "With iA now positioned as Canada’s largest independent wealth management firm, we are establishing a new standard in client service and advisor support by elevating the value proposition for advisors and deploying innovative, industry-leading platforms powered by artificial intelligence. I am confident in Julie’s ability to lead a high-performing executive team, attract and develop advisory teams, and continue to build on Richardson Wealth’s unique culture, trusted reputation and strong company values."

Ms. Gallagher’s career spans more than 20 years of progressively senior roles in the financial services industry. More recently, she was Senior Vice-President and Head, Investment Products & Solutions and Capital Markets, at iA Wealth. Over the past several months her responsibilities have expanded to support Richardson Wealth’s advisory teams.

"I am thrilled by the opportunities ahead as we enter a dynamic new chapter for our combined organization," said Julie Gallagher. "It is a privilege to succeed Dave Kelly, an exceptional leader who has helped lay a strong foundation for the firm’s future. I am also honoured to continue working alongside the Richardson Wealth advisory teams and to further strengthen the exceptional culture they have built."

Richardson Wealth is known for providing comprehensive, client-focused financial advisory services tailored to the high-net-worth market. Its advisors maintain their operational independence while enjoying the benefits of scale and the significant growth opportunities through this acquisition.

Story Continues

About iA Financial Group

iA Financial Group is one of the largest insurance and wealth management groups in Canada, with operations in the United States. Founded in 1892, it is an important Canadian public company and is listed on the Toronto Stock Exchange under the ticker symbol IAG (common shares).

To learn more about iA Financial Group, you can sign up for our newsletter on our website at ia.ca

iA Financial Group is a business name and trademark of iA Financial Corporation Inc.

title: IAMGOLD’s Côté Gold Shift Puts Cash Generation And Buybacks In Focus
content:
Make better investment decisions with Simply Wall St's easy, visual tools that give you a competitive edge.

IAMGOLD (TSX:IMG) has brought the Côté Gold mine into full production, supporting record operational performance over the past year. The company reports strong free cash flow generation that has been used to reduce debt and fund a new share buyback program. Institutional investor interest has increased alongside these operational and financial milestones.

If you follow gold producers, Côté Gold hitting full production is a key development for IAMGOLD (TSX:IMG). The mine is now contributing to record margins, a different cost profile, and substantial free cash flow. All of these factors are relevant if you care about how resilient a producer can be through commodity cycles. For a miner, having a large asset move from the build phase to full output often changes the way the market looks at its risk and balance sheet.

What stands out here is how IAMGOLD is using this progress, with debt reduction and a share buyback signaling a focus on capital structure and shareholder returns. The higher institutional ownership also indicates that larger investors are paying closer attention, which can influence liquidity and trading behavior around the stock. As you think about your own approach to gold exposure, Côté Gold and these capital moves are likely to be central to any view on IAMGOLD going forward.

The move to full production at Côté Gold changes IAMGOLD’s profile from a development-heavy story to one where cash generation and capital allocation sit in the foreground. With record margins and free cash flow, the business model is now more weighted toward operating performance and cost discipline, rather than construction risk. The decision to use that cash to pay down debt and start a share buyback indicates that management is prioritising balance sheet strength and per share metrics, rather than solely pushing the next growth project.

How This Fits Into The IAMGOLD Narrative

Côté Gold reaching full production and contributing to record margins is directly aligned with the narrative that higher output and cost optimisation can support stronger cash flow and earnings potential. The focus on share buybacks could challenge the earlier emphasis on reinvesting free cash flow into projects like Côté Gosselin or Boto Gold if capital returns limit funds available for future growth. Rising institutional interest and index prominence, while important for liquidity, are not fully captured in the existing narrative that centres mainly on production, ESG position, and balance sheet evolution.

Story Continues

The Risks and Rewards Investors Should Consider

⚠️ Profit margins of 23.3% are lower than the 50.2% reported previously, so sustaining record cash generation is not guaranteed if costs remain elevated. ⚠️ Analysts have flagged 2 key risks overall, including concerns that higher operating and capital costs could pressure returns if production or gold prices soften. 🎁 Earnings are currently forecast to grow 15% per year, which is consistent with Côté Gold now running at full capacity and contributing more meaningfully to the group. 🎁 The company is trading at 20.2% below one estimate of fair value, and analysts are in agreement that the share price could rise by 23.3%, which some investors may view as an appealing setup.

What To Watch Going Forward

From here, the key thing to watch is execution around Côté Gold’s cost profile and reliability of output, because any sustained issues there would ripple through free cash flow, debt reduction, and the size of the buyback. You may also want to track how management allocates incremental cash between de-leveraging, advancing projects like Boto Gold, and further shareholder returns. Finally, monitor how IAMGOLD compares with peers such as Barrick Gold and Newmont on production growth, costs, and balance sheet strength, as that context often shapes how the market values mid tier producers like IAMGOLD.

title: Investing.com’s stocks of the week
content:
Investing.com – It’s been an extremely turbulent week for equities.

With the Middle East conflict ongoing and showing no signs of slowing, investors are assessing its length, severity, and potential economic impact on the global economy.

This week’s volatility has resulted in some big moves. Here are Investing.com’s stocks of the week:

Memory Stocks

Memory names such as Sandisk, Western Digital, Micron and Seagate made headlines through 2025 and the beginning of this year following significant rallies. However, they have given back some gains this week.

In the last week, Sandisk has fallen by over 11%, Western Digital is down 7.9%, Micron dropped 4%, and fell Seagate 8.8%.

Airline Stocks

Airline names globally have been significantly impacted by the Middle East conflict.

American Airlines has plunged 17.1% in the last week, while Delta is down 13.4%, United Airlines has declined 18.5%, Southwest and Alaska Air Group have tumbled 20%, and JetBlue has plunged 22%.

European/UK names were also hit, with British Airways owner IAG down 20.5%, while EasyJet and Lufthansa fell around 13%.

Day One Biopharmaceuticals

Day One Biopharmaceuticals shares surged over 65% on Friday following news that the company will be acquired by Servier, an independent international pharmaceutical group.

The company will acquire Day One for $21.50 per share in cash, representing a total equity value of approximately $2.5 billion. The transaction is expected to close in the second quarter of 2026.

Marvell Technology

Marvell also rose on Friday, albeit up around 22% (as of 13:45 ET). The rally followed the company’s quarterly earnings release that saw it top consensus expectations, while also providing positive guidance.

Following the report, William Blair analyst Sebastien Naji reiterated an Outperform rating on the stock.

“Despite lingering questions around the scope and size of individual ASIC engagements, we believe Marvell is well positioned to grab its fair share of the AI infrastructure wallet with a broad portfolio of interconnect, custom ASIC, switching, and storage solutions,” the analyst wrote in a reaction note.

“Given the outlook for accelerating revenue growth over the coming quarters, we remain constructive on shares.”

The Trade Desk

The Trade Desk shares jumped more than 18% on Thursday, and despite a pullback on Friday, remain up over 23% in the last week.

Thursday’s climb followed rumors surrounding a potential partnership with OpenAI.

Following the reports, Evercore ISI analyst Mark Mahaney wrote in a note: “Relative to TTD’s 2025 gross spend of $13.4B, we believe Generative AI engines could be a significant source of incremental gross spend (e.g, press reports indicate OpenAI’s ambitions to drive $25B of annual revenue from advertising by 2030).”

Story Continues

However, Wedbush analyst Alicia Reese was more cautious, downgrading the stock to Underperform, stating: “We do not believe the incremental value of the rumored deal warrants the current share price.”

title: Can Centerra Gold Sustain Growth in a Strong Gold Market?
content:
Centerra Gold Inc. CGAU reported fourth-quarter 2025 revenue of about $401.6 million, representing a 33% increase from the prior-year quarter. The improvement was mainly driven by higher realized prices for both gold and copper, which more than offset the decline in metal sales volumes. Consolidated quarterly output reached 70,853 ounces of gold and about 13 million pounds of copper in the quarter, with Mount Milligan and Oksut mines remaining key contributors.

During the fourth quarter, the company sold 68,143 ounces of gold at an average realized price of $3,415 per ounce compared with 83,876 ounces sold at $2,207 per ounce in the prior-year quarter. Copper sales totaled 12.5 million pounds at an average realized price of $4.69 per pound in the quarter compared with 16.4 million pounds sold at $2.88 per pound a year ago.

The surge in commodity prices supported overall revenue growth despite lower volumes. Centerra also continued to benefit from disciplined cost management and optimization efforts at its core operations.

Among peers, IAMGOLD Corporation IAG reported revenue of $1.09 billion for the fourth quarter of 2025, marking a sharp 130% increase from $469.9 million in the fourth quarter of 2024. IAMGOLD continued to benefit from operational improvements and higher mill throughput at the Cote mine, ongoing optimization efforts and improved mining efficiencies across its portfolio.

Agnico Eagle Mines Limited AEM reported strong revenue of approximately $3.56 billion, representing a sharp increase from roughly $2.22 billion in the fourth quarter of 2024. The growth was primarily driven by a much stronger realized gold price environment, as the company benefited from an average realized gold price of around $4,163 per ounce during the quarter.

Agnico Eagle maintained solid production levels in the quarter, supported by stable output from key operations such as Canadian Malartic, Detour Lake and LaRonde. Agnico Eagle also benefited from its high-quality, low-risk mining jurisdictions, mine optimization, disciplined cost management and productivity improvements.

The Zacks Rundown for CGAU

Shares of CGAU have popped 220% over the past year compared with the industry’s 142.4% rise.Zacks Investment Research

From a valuation perspective, CGAU is currently trading at a forward 12-month price-to-sales of 2.92X, below the industry’s average of 3.41X. It carries a Value Score of B.

title: Zacks Industry Outlook Highlights Agnico Eagle Mines, Franco-Nevada, Equinox Gold, IAMGOLD and Eldorado Gold
content:
For Immediate Release

Chicago, IL – March 6, 2026 – Today, Zacks Equity Research discusses Agnico Eagle Mines Ltd. AEM, Franco-Nevada Corp. FNV, Equinox Gold EQX, IAMGOLD IAG and Eldorado Gold EGO.

Industry: Gold Mining

Link: https://www.zacks.com/commentary/2872203/5-gold-stocks-to-buy-to-ride-solid-price-demand-trends

The outlook for the Zacks Mining - Gold industry looks bright, supported by strong gold price momentum and resilient demand. Gold has surged 18% so far in 2026, following a record-breaking 2025 when prices averaged $3,431 per ounce. Prices are currently around $5,140 per ounce amid geopolitical tensions. Structural supply constraints, including declining output from aging mines and limited new discoveries, are expected to support prices.

Against this backdrop, companies like Agnico Eagle Mines Ltd., Franco-Nevada Corp.,Equinox Gold, IAMGOLD and Eldorado Gold remain well-positioned for growth, aided by strong balance sheets, cost-control initiatives and ongoing growth projects.

About the Industry

The Zacks Mining - Gold industry comprises companies engaged in extracting gold from mines. These mines may either be underground or open pits. Mining is a long and complex process and requires significant financial resources. It involves exploring to evaluate a deposit's size; assessing ways to extract and process ore efficiently, safely and responsibly; and developing the mine before the actual mining process.

It usually takes 10-20 years for a gold mine to produce material that can finally be refined. Nowadays, industry players use a range of sophisticated techniques to extract gold and convert it into dore bars, an alloy of gold and silver, alongside other impurities. These are then sent for purification, after which gold is purchased as bars or coins, or used in jewelry or other purposes.

Major Trends Shaping the Future of the Mining-Gold Industry

Unprecedented Rally in Gold Prices to Aid Industry: Gold recorded a remarkable run in 2025, with the highest annual average of $3,431 per ounce, which was up 44% year over year. Gold price even breached the $4,500 mark in December 2025. The rally was driven by escalating geopolitical and economic uncertainty, a weaker U.S. dollar and continued strong central bank purchases. Total gold demand also hit an all-time high of 5,002 tons.

Investment demand was a major driver, reaching a landmark level of 2,175 tons. Investors seeking safe haven and diversification piled into gold ETFs, adding 801 tons throughout the year. Global bar and coin demand is reaching 12-year highs at 1,374 tons. Central bank buying remained elevated in 2025, with the official sector adding 863t of gold. The upward momentum has continued in 2026, with gold up 18.4% year to date and trading near a record $5,140 per ounce as investors increasingly turn to safe-haven assets amid escalating tensions in the Middle East.

Story Continues

Cost Management Efforts Sustain Margins: The industry has been facing a shortage of skilled workforce, causing a spike in wages. Industry players are grappling with escalating production costs, including electricity, water, and material. Since the industry cannot control gold prices, it focuses on improving the sales volume and the operating cash flow, as well as lowering unit net cash costs. The industry participants are opting for alternative energy sources, such as solar or wind farms, to minimize fuel-price volatility and secure supply. Miners are committed to cost-reduction strategies and digital innovation to drive operating efficiencies.

Structural Supply Constraints to Support Long-Term Prices: Depleting resources, declining output from aging mines and the lack of new mines remain inherent threats to the industry. Given the high costs and risks and lengthy time period associated with developing new mines, many companies prefer to expand their reserve base through mergers and acquisitions.

On the demand side, the use of gold in energy, healthcare and technology is rising. India and China account for around 50% of consumer gold demand. Gold demand in India will remain strong on improving economic momentum and consumer confidence.

The yellow metal has long been considered a safe-haven investment amid financial or political uncertainty. Central banks have been ramping up reserves held in gold due to currency depreciation and geopolitical and economic risks. Therefore, there will be an eventual demand-supply imbalance, which will likely drive gold prices.

Zacks Industry Rank Indicates Bright Prospects

The group’s Zacks Industry Rank, the average of the Zacks Rank of all the member stocks, indicates encouraging near-term prospects. The Zacks Mining - Gold Industry, which is a 40-stock group within the broader Zacks Basic Materials sector, currently carries a Zacks Industry Rank #22, which places it in the top 9% of 243 Zacks industries. Our research shows that the top 50% of the Zacks-ranked industries outperform the bottom 50% by a factor of more than 2 to 1.

Before we present a few stocks that you may want to consider for your portfolio, let us take a look at the industry’s recent stock-market performance and valuation picture.

Industry Versus S&P 500 & Sector

The Mining-Gold Industry has outperformed the sector and the Zacks S&P 500 composite over the past year. The stocks in the industry have collectively grown 145.1% compared with the broader sector’s rise of 46.6%. The S&P 500 has gained 21.6% in the same timeframe.

Industry's Current Valuation

On the basis of the trailing 12-month EV/EBITDA, a commonly used multiple for valuing gold-mining companies, we see that the industry is currently trading at 11.82X compared with the S&P 500’s 17.33X and the Basic Material sector’s trailing 12-month EV/EBITDA of 17.38X.

Over the last five years, the industry traded as high as 12.90X and as low as 5.26X, the median being 7.78X.

5 Mining-Gold Stocks to Bet On

Agnico Eagle Mines: The company continues to prioritize projects aimed at strengthening production and cash-flow generation. It is advancing its key pipeline projects, including the Odyssey project in the Canadian Malartic Complex, Detour Lake, Hope Bay, Upper Beaver and San Nicolas. AEM is also reassessing some higher potential portfolio projects (Hammond Reef, Timmins East and Northern Territory) in light of the high gold price environment.

The company generated a record free cash flow of around $4.4 billion in 2025. Through a disciplined, phased development approach, Agnico Eagle aims to lift annual gold output by 20–30% over the next decade, with the potential to surpass 4 million ounces in the early 2030s. AEM shares have gained 51% over the past six months.

The Zacks Consensus Estimate for this Toronto, Canada-based company’s earnings for fiscal 2026 has moved up 33.5% over the past 60 days. The estimate indicates 60.4% year-over-year growth. AEM has a trailing four-quarter earnings surprise of 10.8%. Agnico Eagle currently sports a Zacks Rank #1 (Strong Buy).

You can see the complete list of today’s Zacks #1 Rank stocks here.

Franco-Nevada: The company recently agreed to a AUD170 million ($120 million) gross royalty from Minerals 260 to support the development of the Bullabulling Gold Project in Western Australia. This deal will lift Franco-Nevada’s effective royalty on the project land package to 2.45%. Bullabulling is a large and growing resource and one of the most attractive gold development projects in Australia.

Franco-Nevada also inked a deal to acquire a $250 million net smelter return royalty from i-80 Gold Corp. The royalty rate will start at 1.5%, increasing to 3.0% beginning in 2031, and will apply to all of i80 Gold’s material properties, including their six projects in various stages of development. This increases FNV’s long-term gold exposure in Nevada, where its royalty coverage already extends to Goldstrike, Gold Quarry, Arthur, Marigold, Bald Mountain, South Arturo and numerous other properties.

Backed by a diversified royalty and streaming portfolio, a debt-free balance sheet and strong cash flows, Franco-Nevada remains well positioned for long-term growth. FNV shares have gained 32.8% over the past six months.

The Zacks Consensus Estimate for this company’s earnings for fiscal 2026 indicates 58.6% year-over-year growth. The estimate has moved up 24% over the past 60 days. FNV has a trailing four-quarter earnings surprise of 7.7%, on average. The company has a long-term estimated earnings growth of 8.9% and currently sports a Zacks Rank of 1.

Equinox Gold: 2025 marked a transformative year for the company with the merger with Calibre creating a tier one North American-focused gold producer anchored by two new long-life Canadian mines. The company also reported delivery of the first gold and commercial production at Valentine ahead of schedule, and portfolio optimization through asset divestments. The company achieved a record production of 922,827 ounces in 2025. EQX has also transformed the balance sheet, reducing debt by more than $1.1 billion since the second quarter of 2025.

In 2026, the company aims to generate strong free cash flow supported by expected gold production of 700,000–800,000 ounces and elevated prices, and continue lowering debt levels. A strengthened balance sheet provides greater flexibility to fund 400,000-500,000 ounces of potential annual organic growth over the next five years from the Phase 2 expansion at Valentine, the Castle Mountain expansion, and optionality at Los Filos. EQX shares have gained 74.5% over the past six months.

Headquartered in Vancouver, Canada, Equinox Gold engages in the exploration, acquisition, development and operation of mineral properties in the Americas. The Zacks Consensus Estimate for EQX’s 2026 earnings has moved up 30% over the past 90 days. The consensus estimate indicates a substantial year-over-year improvement of 263%. EQX has a trailing four-quarter earnings surprise of 89.2%, on average. The company currently sports a Zacks Rank of 1.

IAMGOLD: The company delivered record margins and cash flow in 2025 as its mines moved into robust production phases, as well as solid gold market conditions. Solid operating performance enabled IAMGOLD to advance its balance-sheet deleveraging strategy and return capital to shareholders through its share repurchase program. In 2026, IAMGOLD remains focused on growing the value of its assets while maintaining operational discipline.

The buyback program will continue, supported by cash flows from the Essakane mine. At the Côté operation, the focus will be on reducing unit costs and preparing for a future expansion targeting the combined Côté and Gosselin deposits. The company is also advancing its organic growth pipeline with a comprehensive drill program at its newly consolidated Nelligan Complex, which is already positioning itself as among the largest pre-production projects in Canada. IAG shares have gained 126% over the past six months.

The Zacks Consensus Estimate for this Toronto, Canada-based company’s earnings for fiscal 2026 has moved up 29.7% over the past 60 days. The estimate suggests year-over-year growth of 74%. IAG has a trailing four-quarter earnings surprise of 15.75%, on average. The company currently has a long term estimated earnings growth of 5.9% and sports a Zacks Rank of 1.

Eldorado Gold: The company expects gold production at 490,000-590,000 ounces in 2026, representing a 11% year-over-year increase. This will be supported by its current operating mines, the Lamaque Complex, Kisladag, Efemcukuru and Olympias. First concentrate from the Skouries project is now expected in the early part of the third quarter of 2025, with commercial production targeted for the fourth quarter.

Once operational, Skouries is set to significantly enhance Eldorado’s production profile, costs and cash generation. The company thus expects gold output to reach 620,000-720,000 ounces in 2027, indicating a solid 40% increase from 2025 levels. With higher production and the addition of copper output from Skouries, Eldorado expects a strong increase in free cash flow supported by its stable operating portfolio. EGO shares have gained 58.3% over the past six months.

The Zacks Consensus Estimate for Vancouver, Canada-based Eldorado Gold’s 2026 earnings indicates year-over-year growth of 194.3%. The estimate has moved up 21% over the past 60 days. EGO presently sports a Zacks Rank of 1.

title: Borrowing costs surge on fears of inflation crisis
content:
Energy Secretary Ed Miliband admitted in the House of Commons that British households were “exposed” to rising energy prices - Parliament.tv

Britain’s borrowing costs are surging as economists warned Britain is particularly exposed to an inflation crisis from the war in Iran.

The yield on 10-year gilts – a benchmark for the cost of servicing the national debt – rose 10 basis points on Thursday.

UK borrowing costs are now at their highest level since February 9, when bond markets reacted to speculation over the future of Sir Keir Starmer’s leadership. It puts them on course for the steepest weekly rise since since May 2023, when traders questioned the Bank of England’s ability to keep a lid on inflation.

It came amid a fresh stock market sell off in London and on Wall Street, with the FTSE 100 closing 1.45pc lower.

Investors are rattled by escalating conflict in the Middle East, fearing the war will strangle energy supplies and push up prices.

Ed Miliband admitted that British households were “exposed” to rising global energy prices after the Iran conflict hit oil and gas supplies.

The Energy Secretary said the country had been left at the mercy of international energy markets after a dramatic drop-off in traffic through the vital Strait of Hormuz.

Around a fifth of the world’s oil and gas passes through the trade route, which has been effectively closed after attacks on tankers.

Mr Miliband told MPs on Thursday that tanker movements had “declined very significantly” through the vital waterway after Iran targeted energy production and infrastructure.

He said that “as when Russia invaded Ukraine, we will be exposed to price competition” when the next Ofgem price cap is calculated from July.

Oil prices jumped as the war in Iran entered its sixth day. A barrel of Brent crude, the international standard, rose 3.8pc to $84.52, up from around $70 last week.

Michael Saunders, a former Bank of England rate setter, warned that Britain was particularly vulnerable to an energy shock because of its reliance on imports.

Mr Saunders said inflation was likely to remain higher for longer, warning that it could be closer to 3pc than the Bank’s 2pc target by the end of the year.

Mr Saunders said he expected any “sustained energy price surge” to prompt the government to step in to help pay household energy bills given that “the government’s popularity is low and its political strategy is heavily focused on ‘cost of living’ issues.”

Mr Miliband also hinted the government would consider financial support for households if needed.

Stressing that the impact on households would “depend on the length of time for which this crisis lasts”, he added: “As we continue to monitor the effects of these events, the House and the country should be in no doubt that the Prime Minister’s number one priority is to tackle the cost of living crisis that affects families across Britain, however long this crisis lasts.”

Story Continues

UK borrowing costs had been falling in recent weeks. However, the war has prompted a sustained increase which risks a headache for Rachel Reeves by piling further pressure on the public finances.

The yield on 10-year gilts has jumped from 4.23pc last Friday to 4.54pc by Thursday evening.

Airlines including EasyJet and British Airways owner IAG were among the worst hit on the FTSE 100 on Thursday as jet fuel prices rose to a four year high.

In Europe, France’s CAC 40 declined 1.7pc, and Germany’s DAX lost 1.8pc, while on Wall Street the Dow Jones Industrial Average shed 2pc, the S&P 500 fell 1.3pc and the Nasdaq dropped 1.3pc.

08:52pm

Signing off...

That’s all from us today, but we’ll be back tomorrow with the latest market updates. In the meantime, you can read our latest business and economics news here.

08:43pm

‘If they rise, they rise’

Donald Trump has said he is not concerned about rising petrol prices as the war in Iran sends energy costs soaring.

Asked about higher pump prices, the US president told Reuters: “I don’t have any concern about it.

“They’ll drop very rapidly when this is over, and if they rise, they rise, but this is far more important than having gasoline prices go up a little bit.”

His comments were a stark shift from his election campaign messaging when he promised to cut American energy bills in half, and his recent State of the Union address when he touted lower petrol prices.

08:22pm

$2 trillion deficit blow

The US deficit will be $2tn larger over the next decade as a result of the Supreme Court’s ruling that the majority of Donald Trump’s trade tariffs were illegal, according to the Congressional Budget Office.

The CBO calculated that the ruling against the US President’s use of the International Emergency Economic Powers Act (IEEPA) to impose tariffs will increase America’s primary deficit by $1.6tn and add $0.4tn to its debt interest bill between 2026 and 2036.

IEEPA was the legal justification used for the sweeping “reciprocal” tariffs Mr Trump imposed this year. Charges imposed using IEEPA made up half of the president’s tariff revenues.

But the administration was forced to terminate these levies after the Supreme Court ruled in February that the president could not use IEEPA to impose tariffs without backing from Congress.

The extra costs come as the war in Iran has sent US government borrowing costs soaring, driving up debt interest bills and bringing fresh pressure for the public finances.

07:53pm

5 Hormuz crossings

Just five ships transited through the Strait of Hormuz on Wednesday, according to data company Windward.

This followed just four transits on Tuesday and was down by 94pc compared to the previous week’s daily average.

Iran is still actively attacking ships after it declared it would close the narrow shipping channel.

UK Maritime Trade Operations reported an explosion on a crude tanker anchored southeast of Kuwait yesterday, which was the most northern attack on a ship since the war in Iran began.

07:35pm

US government borrowing costs soar

American government borrowing costs have surged as higher energy prices bring fresh inflation fears.

Yields on 10-year Treasuries jumped by another five basis points on Thursday to hit 4.13pc, up from 3.96pc before the war in Iran began.

Rates on two-year notes rose by another four basis points to hit 3.59pc, their highest level since late January. Yields on two-year Treasuries are now up 21 basis points since Friday.

Investors are becoming increasingly worried that soaring oil and gas prices will soon be passed through to businesses and consumers, driving up prices in a fresh threat to the Federal Reserve’s 2pc inflation target.

07:27pm

US waives Rosneft sanctions in Germany

The US has waived sanctions on a major German refinery that is owned by the sanctioned Russian oil major Rosneft as the war in Iran squeezes global energy markets.

The Treasury’s Office of Foreign Assets Control (Ofac) on Thursday granted a license to the German arm of Rosneft, exempting it from US sanctions against the Russian oil company announced in October.

This means that German companies are now allowed to transact indefinitely with the three refineries in Germany that Rosneft holds shares in, including the PCK oil-processing plant in Schwedt which supplies the vast majority of Berlin’s petrol.

Previously, Rosneft’s German subsidiaries had been operating under a temporary six-month exemption, which was due to expire at the end of April.

The OFAC announcement comes days after German Chancellor Friedrich Merz met with Donald Trump at the White House.

07:07pm

Greek seafarer strike

Greek seafarers went on a 24-hour strike on Thursday in protest over crews stranded in the Gulf because of the Iran war.

Greece’s PNO union called for the immediate evacuation and repatriation of all seafarers in the region

The International Maritime Organisation said about 20,000 seafarers are stuck in the region, with at least 200 ships anchored near the Strait of Hormuz, unable to pass through after Iran declared it would open fire on any vessel trying to transit.

The International Bargaining Forum (IBF), a central negotiating body for maritime workers and employers, agreed on Thursday that seafarers could refuse to sail into the Gulf region and have a right to receive higher pay and double compensation if they are harmed.

06:13pm

Fresh volatility hits markets

The escalating conflict in the Middle East caused fresh market volatility in markets across the world.

In Europe, France’s CAC 40 declined 1.7pc, and Germany’s DAX lost 1.8pc.

At lunchtime in New York, the Dow Jones Industrial Average shed 2pc, the S&P 500 fell 1.3pc and the Nasdaq dropped 1.3pc.

South Korea’s Kospi rose 10pc on Thursday, recovering most of Wednesday’s record 12pc drop. The index has been under pressure this week due to the country’s dependence on imported oil.

05:44pm

Middle East conflict to unleash new era of soaring air fares

Holidaymakers face a new era of rising airfares as war in the Middle East pushes up fuel prices and shuts airports.

The price of jet fuel, the single biggest part of an airline’s costs, has surged to a four-year high after the US hit Iran last week.

Airlines typically “hedge” their fuel costs to stop wild price fluctuations but experts say airlines could be forced to increase fares for passengers once those hedging protections expire.

Carriers traversing the Middle East are also having to make diversions of an hour or more to avoid skies made unsafe by the conflict – adding to airlines’ fuel costs. Michael Stevenson/Michael Stevenson

05:05pm

London markets close

Investor nerves over the escalating conflict in the Middle East jolted markets.

London-listed stocks declined, while oil prices continued to rise on Thursday. A barrel of Brent crude, the international standard, rose 3.8pc to $84.52, up from around $70 last week.

The FTSE 100 fell 1.5pc, or 153.71 points, to 10,413.94.

The more domestically-focused FTSE 250 shed 0.7pc, or 148.19 points, to 22,748.48.

04:55pm

Government borrowing costs on the rise in Europe

Government borrowing costs have risen in several countries across Europe, as concerns of an inflation shock from escalating conflict in the Middle East grow.

German 10-year Bund yields rose 2.84pc their highest since February 9. They were up from 2.64pc on Monday and are on track for the largest weekly rise since March 2025.

The yield on 10-year French bonds climbed from 3.22pc on Monday to 3.44pc on Thursday.

Chris Turner, head of global markets at ING, said: “High energy prices have seen short-dated yields spike again today – but this is a global and not just a euro zone phenomenon.”

04:32pm

Bank of England likely to hold rates, says Oxford Economics

Rate setters at the Bank of England are likely to hold interest rates for now, according to Oxford Economics, amid rising energy prices.

Michael Saunders, a senior adviser at Oxford Economics, said the Bank’s Monetary Policy Committee (MPC) is unlikely to cut rates at its next meeting on 18 March.

He added: “If the surge in energy prices persists or expands, the MPC will be set for an extended pause.

“Given that policy remains somewhat restrictive, the Committee is unlikely to hike rates unless inflation expectations rise significantly.”

Rate setters at Threadneedle Street voted to hold borrowing costs at 3.75pc in their most recent meeting in February.

03:48pm

The UK is ‘vulnerable’ to energy prices driving up inflation

The UK is particularly vulnerable to increases in inflation driven by rising energy prices, a former Bank of England rate setter has warned.

Michael Saunders, a senior adviser at Oxford Economics and former member of the Bank of England’s Monetary Policy Committee, said: “The Eurozone and the UK are relatively vulnerable to potential second round effects from the energy price surge triggered by the Iran conflict”.

He added that the UK’s exposure to climbing wholesale gas prices means that the rate of inflation is likely to be impacted.

Oxford Economics has increased its forecasts for the UK’s CPI inflation by 0.5 percentage points in the final three months of 2026.

The energy regulator, Ofgem, sets the price cap each quarter so if the recent rises in energy costs are maintained, they will begin to impact household electricity and gas bills in July.

Mr Saunders said: “The likelihood is relatively high that a sustained energy price surge would prompt the government to provide fiscal support given that the government’s popularity is low and its political strategy is heavily focused on ‘cost of living’ issues.”

03:27pm

Slower rate cuts more likely than rises, say economists

The Bank of England is more likely to cut rates at a slower pace than resort to raising them, a group of economists said.

Capital Economics said the surge in energy prices this week “has been large, but so far it has been nowhere near that seen in 2022 after Russia’s invasion of Ukraine”.

Oil prices have jumped by about 15pc to around $84 per barrel while UK natural gas prices have surged by about 73pc to 136 pence per therm.

That compares to a surge of 147pc to $128 for Brent crude and 1,035pc to 640p per therm for gas between January 2021 and their peaks in 2022.

Even if Brent crude averaged $100 per barrel over the next year and UK natural gas prices average 250p per therm, inflation would rise by 1.3 percentage points, Capital Economics said, which is lower than the 3.9 point boost in October 2022.

Deputy chief UK economist Ruth Gregory said: “We are not yet at the stage where we are changing our forecast that the Bank will cut interest rates from 3.75pc now to 3pc by the end of this year.

“But the risk is that higher inflation delays rate cuts this year or prevents rates from being cut at all.”

03:16pm

Europe and Asia ‘hardest hit’ by global energy shock

Europe and Asia would be “hardest hit” by a surge in oil and gas prices despite efforts to reduce reliance on imports, according to British investment manager Charles Stanley.

Chief investment officer Patrick Farrell said the two continents were still at risk despite efforts particularly to wean economies off Russian gas following the supply shock after the invasion of Ukraine in 2022.

He said the market reaction so far to the Iran war “has been proportionate” despite bond yields soaring and stocks falling.

He said: “Global energy systems are better positioned today due to the diversification efforts undertaken in recent years, and markets appear less inclined to extrapolate worst-case scenarios.

“Europe and Asia would be the hardest hit should energy prices remain elevated, as both regions are major net importers of oil and gas.

“Nonetheless, the structural improvements made since 2022 mean governments and industries are better equipped to manage such supply disruptions.”

03:01pm

Banks raise mortgage rates as war in Iran hits borrowing costs

The first major lenders have raised mortgage rates as the conflict in the Middle East and a potential inflation shock push up the price of borrowing, writes Zachariah Sharif and Madeleine Ross.

HSBC, Natwest and Coventry Building Society announced their fixed rates would increase from this week. Challenger lender, Gen H, said it had reversed a cut because of “global uncertainty”.

Before the outbreak of the war last weekend, markets had been expecting the Bank of England to cut the Bank Rate – currently 3.75pc – twice more this year, with an estimated 85pc chance of a reduction in March.

02:36pm

US stocks slump over inflation fears

Wall Street opened lower over the looming threat of inflation from the deepening energy crisis in the Middle East.

The Dow Jones Industrial Average fell 0.6pc to 48,467.10 while the benchmark S&P 500 dropped by 0.2pc to 6,854.84.

The tech-heavy Nasdaq Composite declined by 0.2pc to 22,772.48.

02:21pm

Borrowing costs climb as Starmer addresses nation

The cost of government borrowing has pushed higher just as Sir Keir Starmer said he would send more fighter jets to the Middle East.

The yield on UK gilts rose sharply – up 10 basis points – to 5.54pc on Thursday just as the Prime Minister addressed the nation on the Iran war.

Sir Keir said Britain is sending four additional Typhoon jets to join its operations in Qatar.

Bond yields – the return governments promises to buyers of their debt – have risen sharply this week over concerns that a protracted conflict will drive up inflation.

01:54pm

Stocks slump as inflation fears deepen

Global stocks have fallen as the ongoing Middle East conflict raises fears of a spike in inflation.

The benchmark indexes in the UK, France and Germany all turned lower in afternoon trading, while markets on Wall Street were also on track to open lower.

Traders are betting that the surge in oil and gas prices will complicate future decisions on interest rates for the US Federal Reserve, the Bank of England and other central banks.

Wall Street’s main indexes have fared better than their European and Asian counterparts this week, aided primarily by a rebound in technology stocks that bore the brunt of a sell-off in February.

However, prolonged disruption to shipping through the strategic Strait of Hormuz is likely to further fuel inflation pressures through energy and shipping costs.

It comes on top of Donald Trump’s new 15pc global tariffs, which US treasury secretary Scott Bessent said would come into effect at some point this week.

In premarket trading on Wall Street, the Dow Jones Industrial Average was down 0.6pc, the S&P 500 fell 0.3pc and the tech-heavy Nasdaq 100 was down 0.4pc.

01:29pm

Climate crisis is biggest threat to security, says Miliband

Ed Miliband continued to defend his policy on oil and gas after Donald Trump this week urged the Government to “open up the North Sea”.

The Energy Secretary told the Commons that new North Sea exploration licences “are completely marginal to that basin and would make no difference to the prices set by international markets and paid by UK billpayers”.

He later said: “This Government has taken the decision to keep existing fields open for their lifetime, including through transitional energy certificates in our North Sea future plan as called for and welcomed by industry, and also the decision not to issue new licenses to explore new fields as the science tells us the right thing to do, because this Government will not ignore the biggest long-term threat multiplier to our security and stability that we face – the climate crisis.”

Mr Miliband added: “The lesson of Russia’s invasion of Ukraine and the lesson of recent days is that the only route to greater energy independence for Britain is our mission and we will double down on it, because every solar panel we install, every wind turbine we put up, every nuclear power station we build makes us more secure as a country and protects families and businesses from these kind of energy shocks.”

He said: “In an unstable world, we will keep driving for energy sovereignty and abundance with clean, homegrown power – we will not gamble with the British people’s money in the fossil fuel casino and ignore the lessons of the past.”

01:26pm

Miliband insists new North Sea exploration will ‘make no difference’

Ed Miliband insisted new licences for oil and gas exploration would “make no difference” to Britain’s energy production, as prices of oil and gas surge.

The Energy Secretary was accused of having “no plan for when the wind doesn’t blow” by shadow Tory minister Harriet Cross.

Mr Miliband told the Commons it is “on average 10 years from exploration to production”.

He pointed to a report from the National Energy System Operator which said the biggest thing Britain could do for energy security was to “drive towards the clean energy transition”.

“The more we fail to do that, the more that we are exposed given that the North Sea is a declining basin that has seen production fall by 75pc in the last 25 years, and 70,000 jobs lost under them,” he said pointing at the Tory benches.

01:14pm

Miliband says abandoning renewables ‘a dangerous and reckless strategy’

Ed Miliband said conflict in the Middle East has shown why his opponents’ calls to change course on renewable energy “would be such a dangerous and reckless strategy”.

The Energy Secretary told the Commons when he announced the result of a renewables auction earlier this year, he “warned the House that people can have incredibly short memories, given that we’re just four years on from Russia’s invasion of Ukraine”.

Mr Miliband told the Commons: “I warned at the time it was a foolish and dangerous gamble to bet on geopolitical stability at a time of greater geopolitical instability than at any time for generations.

“I warned the Opposition failed to learn the lessons from the Ukraine crisis, which caused the worst cost-of-living crisis in memory, and I warned that a dogma of opposing clean energy would damage this country and risk families and businesses being left to pick up the bill.

“The events of recent days have unfortunately shown why this would be such a dangerous and reckless strategy and we will continue to reject it.”

12:57pm

Consumers at risk of rising energy prices, Miliband admits

The Energy Secretary told the Commons that households would be shielded from rising oil and gas prices only until the start of July.

He said the Ofgem price cap will protect households from April to June, when prices will come down to £1,641.

However, analysts at Cornwall Insight have warned energy bills could jump by £160 a year to £1,801 under the cap mechanism from July.

Mr Miliband told MPs that Britain “must learn the right long-term lessons” from the surge in oil and gas prices since the outbreak of war in the Middle East.

He said the “only route to energy security is to get off our dependence on fossil fuel markets, whose prices we do not control”.

12:53pm

Miliband warns Britain ‘exposed’ to rising energy prices

Ed Miliband said Britain would be “exposed to price competition” on international energy markets as the Iran conflict hits global supplies of oil and gas.

The Energy Secretary said traffic through the vital Strait of Hormuz had “declined very significantly” after Iran targetted energy production and infrastrucure.

He said the UK “benefits from strong and diverse energy supplies, including the North Sea” and insisted he was “confident on security of supply”.

He said Qatar supplied Britain with only 1pc of supplies last year, days after the country closed its biggest natural gas plant following missile strikes.

Speaking in the Commons, he warned MPs that “as when Russia invaded Ukraine, we will be exposed to price competition”.

He said this “reflects our position as a price taker rather than a price maker in these markets”.

12:41pm

Dettol maker focusing on safety of staff in Iran war

The boss of the household goods giant behind Dettol and Harpic has said he is focusing on the safety of its 400-strong workforce in the Middle East as the Iran conflict enters a sixth day.

Reckitt Benckiser chief executive Kris Licht said it was “too early” to say whether supply and prices would be affected by the fast “evolving” situation in the Middle East.

He said the company’s immediate priority was to look after staff safety and well-being across its operations in the region.

The group – whose brands include Strepsils throat sweets and Nurofen painkillers – has closed a factory in Bahrain because of safety reasons and instructed all workers in the region to work from home following the start of the war last week.

Mr Licht said: “Our overwhelming focus at the moment is on the safety and well-being of our employees and their families.”

He added: “It’s significant for the people that work in these markets and live in these communities and we want to do everything we can to support them and their well-being.”

The comments came as the group cautioned that a weak cold and flu season globally and challenging trading in Europe would knock its performance at the start of 2026.

Shares fell more than 6pc to the bottom of the FTSE 100 as the gloomy outlook overshadowed 2025 figures showing pre-tax profits jumped to £3.8bn, up from £2.1bn in 2024 thanks to a 5pc rise in like-for-like sales.

12:30pm

Jet fuel prices hit four-year high in blow for airlines

Jet fuel prices have surged to a four-year high as the conflict in the Middle East pushes up the cost of oil and gas.

The benchmark contract for airline fuel surged by 27pc to $1,269 per tonne on Tuesday, according to the latest data from price agency Argus.

That leaves the northwest Europe price, as it is known, up 80pc compared to a month ago before tensions began rising in the Middle East.

At least four jet fuel tankers were waiting in the Gulf on Tuesday, seemingly unable to exit through the Strait of Hormuz, Argus said.

Iran has threatened to attack ships using the vital trade route, through which a fifth of the world’s oil and gas passes.

Argus head of European jet pricing Amaar Khan said: “The conflict has had a more pronounced effect on jet fuel than on other oil products including diesel, because Europe is most dependent on direct jet fuel imports from the Mideast Gulf.

“At least 40pc of Europe’s jet fuel imports in 2025 came from the Mideast Gulf and used the Strait of Hormuz. Kuwait is Europe’s single-largest jet fuel supplier.”The cost of jet fuel has hit a four-year high after the outbreak of war in the Middle East - REUTERS/Vivek Prakash

12:18pm

Oil prices rise as US and Iran ‘aren’t backing down’

Oil prices remain higher as the fighting rages on in the Middle East.

Brent crude oil was last up 2.2pc at more than $83 a barrel and US-produced West Texas Intermediate was up 2.8pc at nearly $77.

Both main oil contracts have climbed around 14pc since Friday, raising concerns of a fresh spike in inflation and dealing a blow to hopes for lower interest rates.

Jim Reid, an analyst at Deutsche Bank, said: “The geopolitical situation is clearly moving fast, and oil prices are moving up again.

“That comes as both the US and Iran have made clear they aren’t backing down.”

11:50am

BP says staff safety is ‘top priority’

BP said it was “taking all necessary steps” to keep its staff safe after reports it has evacuated staff from the Rumaila ‌oilfield in Iraq.

The oil giant did not deny the reports that foreign workers were being removed.

It said: “People’s safety and wellbeing remain our top priority, and we are taking all necessary steps to support our partners and ensure the safety of our people.

“We are continuously assessing the situation, have been communicating with those people under our duty of care and will address them directly should there be a need for any change.”

11:47am

Lufthansa announces further flight cancellations

Germany’s flagship carrier has announced it will not operate flights to Tehran until at least May as the war in Iran rages on.

Lufthansa also said it had suspended flights to Tel Aviv until at least March 22 and those to and from Beirut until at least March 28.

Flights to and from Abu Dhabi and Dubai will not resume until at least March 10, it said.

11:17am

BP staff evacuated after drone strikes

BP staff have reportedly been evacuated from an oilfield in Iraq after two drone strikes.

Foreign staff have been removed from the British oil giant’s ‌Rumaila site following the attacks, according to Reuters.

11:01am

Long war to push up European inflation, say rate setters

Three ​European Central Bank ‌policymakers have warned that eurozone inflation would likely rise if the war in Iran were to become drawn out.

As the US–Iran war entered its sixth day, the conflict has widened beyond Gulf states and into Asia, convulsing global markets and raising questions about the ECB’s benign outlook for the eurozone.

The ECB’s vice president Luis de Guindos and the central bank governors of Germany and Finland all said it was too ‌early to draw ‌conclusions but warned ⁠that a prolonged, wider war may push up inflation.

“The baseline (is) that this is going to be short-lived,” he told an event in Brussels. “If it is longer, then there is a ⁠risk that inflation expectations will change.”

The ECB was stung by an energy-led rise in inflation following Russia’s invasion of Ukraine in 2022, which it initially wrote off as temporary before hastily raising rates by a record amount.

Finnish governor Olli Rehn said policymakers should not be “overly optimistic” about a swift resolution of the conflict, adding there had already been “quite some escalation”.

Bundesbank President Joachim Nagel said a prolonged ‌conflict ​would push up inflation ⁠and depress growth.

“If the conflict comes to a swift end...the consequences for inflation would be short-term and limited overall,” said the head of ⁠Germany’s Bundesbank.

“By contrast, if energy prices were to remain elevated for an extended period of time, this would tend to ​lead to higher inflation and weaker economic activity in the euro area.”Luis de Guindos, vice president of the European Central Bank, said a prolonged Iran war would change the inflation outlook - Simon Wohlfahrt/Bloomberg

10:29am

Wizz Air ‘more exposed’ to jet fuel costs

Wizz Air will suffer a heavy blow from the Iran conflict because it is more exposed to changes in costs of jet fuel, analysts said.

Investment bank RBC Capital Markets said the Hungarian carrier was not as “highly hedged” against changes in fuel prices as some of its rivals.

It warned other European airlines would also be “negatively impacted by direct exposure to the Middle East conflict”, but not to the same extent as Wizz Air.

As a result, a sudden end to the conflict would offer Wizz a bigger boost, it said.

Analyst Ruairi Cullinane said: “We expect European airline shares to remain sensitive to the geopolitical outlook, duration of the conflict and oil price.

“Conversely, signs that the conflict is de-escalating, or nearing an end, could be positive for European airlines, and particularly those with greater exposure to the conflict like Wizz Air.”

Shares were last down 7.9pc.

10:13am

Travel turmoil increases fears air fares will jump

The turmoil affecting airlines has raised concerns that consumers are about to be hit with increases in air fares.

Deutsche Bank chief UK economist Sanjay Raja said travel fares are “highly sensitive to energy prices”.

Brent crude prices have climbed by more than 14pc so far this week over fears that tankers carrying oil and gas supplies will be unable to pass safely through the Strait of Hormuz along the Iran coast.

Earlier this week, Ryanair chief executive Michael O’Leary said there had been a “big collapse in bookings to the Middle East”.

“I suspect it won’t ⁠go on long and therefore I suspect it won’t have any long-term trends, but I think there’s no doubt that it has undermined confidence in air travel to the Gulf.”

09:45am

Airlines cancel 23,000 flights

The Iran conflict has forced airlines to cancel 23,000 flights since the first strikes by the US at the weekend, according to analytics company Cirium.

Emirates, the largest international airline in the world, said it had extended its suspension of flights to Dubai until Saturday.

The first Government rescue flight was expected to take off later today, having been delayed for “operational reasons” on the ground, according to Home Office minister Alex Norris.

09:27am

Wary consumers could worsen inflation shock

The global economy could suffer an even sharper inflation shock from the Iran war as consumers still reel from the surge in prices since the pandemic, a Wall Street banking giant has warned.

Goldman Sachs warned that the recent rise in oil prices will “boost inflation and slow growth” but said there was a “broader concern is that rising energy prices could de-anchor inflation expectations”.

While a 10pc rise in oil prices has usually pushed up inflation expectations, it said the “effects are slightly larger when oil price shocks are large and much higher when inflation has been elevated”.

Analyst Joseph Briggs said: “Given that the post-pandemic inflation surge remains top of mind for many consumers, inflation expectations are likely more sensitive than normal.”

08:56am

Iran war wipes £3bn off airlines

Nearly £3bn has been wiped off the value of UK-listed airlines this week as the industry braces for further cancellations and rising fuel costs over the Iran war.

The FTSE 100 was dragged lower by 0.2pc in early trading on Thursday, with the mid-cap FTSE 250 lower by 0.3pc, as a profit warning underlined the scale of the challenge facing the sector.

Shares in airlines plunged as Wizz Air said it faced a €50m (£43.5m) blow to its profits from the crisis in the Middle East after it was forced to cancel flights.

The Hungarian carrier, which operates a fleet of 260 planes, issued a profit warning as it faced “adverse movement in macroeconomic factors” such as rising oil and gas prices.

Its shares dropped by as much as 7.9pc as it warned that it expects losses for its current financial year to exceed €25m.

Other airlines were also hit, with easyJet down as much as 4.7pc and British Airways owner IAG dropping by as much as 3.7pc. More than £845m was wiped off UK-listed carriers on Thursday alone.

Oil prices continued their march higher as Iran launched a new wave of attacks at Israeli and American bases.

Brent crude, the international benchmark, was up 3.5pc to more than $84 a barrel, close to the 20-month highs hit during panic selling on Tuesday.

Crude prices rose as Israel said it had begun a “large-scale” attack on Tehran, while China told its largest oil refiners to suspend exports of diesel and gasoline, according to Bloomberg News.

Wizz Air’s poor performance was eclipsed only by recruiter PageGroup, which plunged more than 18pc after it revealed annual profits plunged by 67pc.

European ‌shares were lower amid the deepening Middle East conflict.

The Cac 40 in Paris was down 0.6pc while the Dax in Frankfurt declined by 0.5pc.

08:31am

Wizz Air warns of €50m hit from Iran war

Shares in airlines plunged as Wizz Air said it faced a €50m (£43.5m) blow to its profits from the crisis in the Middle East after it was forced to cancel flights.

The Hungarian carrier, which operates a fleet of 260 planes, issued a profit warning as it faced “adverse movement in macroeconomic factors” such as rising oil and gas prices.

Its shares dropped by as much as 6.2pc as it warned that it expects losses for its current financial year to exceed €25m.

Other airlines were also hit, with easyJet down 3.4pc and British Airways owner IAG dropping by 3pc.

08:25am

Miliband must reopen the North Sea, Octopus boss says

The boss of Britain’s biggest gas and electricity supplier has called on Ed Miliband to reopen the North Sea to strengthen the country’s energy supplies.

Greg Jackson, the founder and chief executive of Octopus, urged the Energy Secretary to exploit Britain’s own fossil fuel reserves after war in the Middle East triggered higher oil and gas prices on global markets.

Writing for The Telegraph, he warned that Britain is “staring down the barrel” of another energy crisis.

“Global gas prices have doubled since Iran effectively closed the Strait of Hormuz, and UK wholesale electricity prices are up about 50pc,” Mr Jackson writes.Greg Jackson, pictured, urged Ed Miliband to exploit Britain’s own fossil fuel reserves - Hollie Adams/Reuters

08:09am

Borrowing costs jump over fears of energy shock

The cost of government borrowing leapt as bond markets opened following the latest spike in the price of oil.

Bond yields, the return governments promise to buyers of their debt, rose across Europe over concerns the war in the Middle East will deliver an inflation shock.

The yield on 10-year UK gilts rose by seven basis points to more than 4.5pc, mirroring sharp rises seen earlier this week.

Eurozone government bonds were headed for their ⁠steepest weekly selloff in a year, with Germany’s benchmark 10-year bund up four basis points to 2.79pc.

Commerzbank analyst Erik Liem said the rise in oil prices could impact the European Central Bank’s (ECB’s) economic projections later this month.

Traders have priced out a rate cut by the Bank of England later this month in response to the inflation threat, with money markets showing no prospect of cuts from the ECB at all this year.

08:02am

UK stocks mixed as oil prices rise

Stock markets in London lacked direction after the latest jump in the price of oil.

The FTSE 100, which features oil gaints Shell and BP, rose by 0.1pc to 10,576.62.

However the FTSE 250, which is more focused on Britain’s economy, declined by 0.1pc to 22,877.94.

07:42am

China faces risks from closure of Strait of Hormuz

The global energy trade is in turmoil as the war in the Middle East chokes off oil and natural gas shipments, causing prices to soar.

Leaders in China are acutely aware of the potential impact, as the world’s largest crude oil importer.

Since the Iran war began, the price of Brent crude, the international standard, has jumped 15pc to about $84 per barrel, hitting its the highest level since July 2024 on Tuesday.

Sustained spikes in oil prices would ripple through their broader economies, straining transport, industry and households.

China is the biggest buyer of Iranian oil, but Beijing has prioritised energy security and has alternatives, including major use of renewables.

It imported about 1.4 million barrels per day from Iran last year, roughly 13pc of its total seaborne crude imports, according to Kpler.

Asia is the most exposed since it relies heavily on imported fuel, much of it shipped through the Strait of Hormuz, the narrow passageway that carries a fifth of global trade in crude oil and liquefied natural gas (LNG).

About 13 million barrels of oil per day moved through the corridor in 2025, according to energy consultancy Kpler. That is about a third of all seaborne crude, the unrefined petroleum that is processed into fuels such as gasoline and diesel.

More than 80pc of the LNG shipped through the strait in 2024 went to Asia, according to the US Energy Information Administration.

Zulfikar Yurnaidi of the Association of Southeast Asian Nations ’ Centre for Energy said: “The crisis, with the closure of the Hormuz Strait as the latest development, would not only raise oil and gas prices but also grind global economic activity to a halt.”Drones were intercepted by air defences at the Fujairah oil industry zone in the UAE - REUTERS/Amr Alfiky

07:17am

Good morning

Thanks for joining me. Oil prices marched higher as Iran launched a new wave of attacks at Israeli and American bases.

Brent crude, the international benchmark, was up 3.5pc to more than $84 a barrel, close to the 20-month highs hit during panic selling on Tuesday.

The rise followed warnings from Iran that the US would “bitterly regret” torpedoing an Iranian warship in the Indian Ocean.

Meanwhile Israel said it had begun a “large-scale” attack on Tehran.

Oil also rose as China told its largest oil refiners to suspend exports of diesel and gasoline, according to Bloomberg News.

China is a net importer of oil and is one of several major Asian economies that depend on the vital Strait of Hormuz trade route, where oil and gas traffic is currently blocked.

The Middle East was the source of 57pc of China’s direct seaborne crude imports in 2025, according to analytics firm Kpler. Here is what you need to know.

5 things to start your day

1) Hiring a young person to cost almost £7,000 more under Labour | Scrapping youth rate of minimum wage will harm employers, think tank warns

2) Man killed himself ‘under orders from Google chatbot’ | Gemini AI allegedly issued four-hour ‘countdown’ for Florida male to take his own life

3) UK risks £3bn blow as US promises 15pc tariffs within days | White House to impose sweeping levies despite Supreme Court decision blow to Trump

4) London-listed oil company evacuates staff from the Middle East | Gulf Marine Services halts maintenance work in Persian Gulf after pulling personnel out

5) Interest rates ‘risk climbing above 4pc’ as Iran war rages | Conflict’s impact on energy prices raises doubts about Threadneedle Street’s appetite for further cuts

What happened overnight

Asian stocks jumped after a three-day rout sparked by the Middle East war.

Seoul was again the standout, with the Kospi soaring 12pc at one point as traders snapped up bargains following the previous two days’ near 20pc collapse that put the index within reach of a bear market.

South Korea’s president ordered on Thursday the activation of a $68bn stabilisation fund as the Middle East crisis roiled markets.

The advances followed a positive day on Wall Street, where forecast-topping figures on US private-sector hiring and services industry activity provided some much-needed positive news.

Global markets have been thrown into turmoil this week after the United States and Israel began strikes against Iran on Saturday, killing its supreme leader and sparking a wave of retaliatory attacks across the Gulf.

Tehran also effectively shut down the Strait of Hormuz, through which a fifth of the world’s crude and considerable liquefied natural gas (LNG) supplies travel, sending prices soaring.

Both main oil contracts are up almost 20pc since Friday, stoking the chances of a fresh spike in inflation and dealing a blow to hopes for lower interest rates.

Among the best Asian performers was Seoul, which had advanced about 50pc since the start of the year - having gained 76pc in 2025 - thanks to a surge in chip giants Samsung and SK Hynix.

There were also gains elsewhere in the region, with Tokyo piling on 1.9pc, while Taipei jumped more than 2pc.

Hong Kong, Shanghai, Sydney, Singapore, Wellington, Manila, Mumbai and Bangkok were also well up though most pared their early rallies.

It came after markets stabilised on Wednesday after reports that Iranian intelligence forces had told the US it was open to talks.

The price of Brent crude rose by 0.45pc to $81.77 per barrel. European gas futures fell by 8pc but were still up 58pc compared to Friday.

Stock markets rose with the US benchmark S&P 500 climbing by 0.79pc. The Nasdaq Composite jumped 1.3pc and the Dow Jones Industrial Average rose by 0.49pc.

title: BofA Raises Its Price Target on IAMGOLD Corporation (IAG) to $27.50 and Maintains a Buy Rating
content:
IAMGOLD Corporation (NYSE:IAG) is among the 10 Fastest Growing NYSE Stocks to Buy.BofA Raises its price target on IAMGOLD Corporation (IAG) to $27.50 and Maintains a Buy Rating

On February 26, 2026, BofA analyst Sathish Kasinathan raised the firm’s price target on IAMGOLD Corporation (NYSE:IAG) to $27.50 from $23 and maintained a Buy rating on the shares. The analyst said the firm updated its price targets for North American metals and mining stocks under coverage after revising its forecasts for metal prices in 2026.

On February 25, 2026, Scotiabank raised its price target on IAMGOLD Corporation (NYSE:IAG) to $25 from $23 and kept a Sector Perform rating on the shares. The firm said it updated its models for gold and precious minerals companies under coverage and noted that the company optimized its Cote Gold operation and delivered on reserves in Q2.

On February 17, 2026, IAMGOLD Corporation (NYSE:IAG) reported its financial and operating results for the fourth quarter and year ended December 31, 2025. President and CEO Renaud Adams said the company delivered another year of strong and safe operating performance, highlighting record margins and cash flow as its mines moved into robust production phases supported by favorable gold market conditions. IAMGOLD reported adjusted EBITDA of $1.5B for 2025, including $710.1M in the fourth quarter.

IAMGOLD Corporation (NYSE:IAG) operates as a gold producer and developer in Canada and Burkina Faso through its subsidiaries.
